10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.

14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.

33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on

93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of

101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}

140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.

155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =

177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).

236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}

283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}

306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}

346	\begin{lemma}\leanok
347	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
351	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
352	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
353	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
354	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
355	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
359	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
360	  %  function
361	  %  $$
362	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
363	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
364	  %  $$
365	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
366	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
367	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
369	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
370	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
372	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
373	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
374	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
375	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
376	  such that for all \(n > N\)
377	  \[
378	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
379	  \]
380	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
381	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
382	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
383	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
384	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
385	
386	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
388	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
389	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
390	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
391	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
392	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
394	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
396	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
397	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
398	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
399	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
400	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
405	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
406	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
407	  %   and axiom-clean. The present theorem is not yet a Lean declaration; the ambient
408	  %   subquotient route (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing
409	  %   graded-quotient API by working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
410	\end{lemma}
411	
412	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
413	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
414	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
415	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
416	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
417	%  following short exact sequence
418	%  $$
419	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
420	%  $$
421	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
422	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
423	\begin{proof}
424	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
425	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
426	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
427	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
429	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
431	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
432	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
433	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
434	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
435	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
436	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
437	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
438	
439	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
440	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
441	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
442	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
443	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
444	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
445	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
446	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
447	  is the number on which we induct.
448	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
449	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
450	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
451	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
452	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
453	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
454	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
455	
456	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
457	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
458	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
459	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
460	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
461	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
462	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
463	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
464	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
465	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
466	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
467	
468	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
469	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
470	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
471	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
472	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
473	  \[
474	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
475	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
476	  \]
477	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
478	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
479	  with
480	  \[
481	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
482	    \qquad (n > N),
483	  \]
484	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
485	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
486	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
487	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
488	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
489	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
490	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
491	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
492	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
493	\end{proof}

495	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
496	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
497	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
498	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
499	
500	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
501	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
502	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
503	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
504	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
505	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
506	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
507	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
508	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
509	
510	\begin{lemma}\leanok
511	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
512	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
513	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
514	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
515	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
516	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
517	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
518	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
519	  \(F\):
520	  \[
521	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
522	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
523	  \]
524	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
525	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
526	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
527	  the Cauchy product.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{lemma}\leanok
531	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
532	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
533	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
534	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
535	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
536	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
537	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
538	  \[
539	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
540	  \]
541	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
542	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
543	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
544	  \[
545	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
546	  \]
547	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
548	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
549	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
550	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
551	\end{lemma}
552	
553	\begin{proof}
554	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
555	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
556	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
557	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
558	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
559	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
560	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
561	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
562	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
563	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
564	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
565	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
566	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
567	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
568	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
569	\end{proof}

571	\begin{definition}\leanok
572	[Rational Hilbert function]
573	  \label{def:ratHilb}
574	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
575	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
576	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
577	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
578	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
579	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
580	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
581	  \[
582	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
583	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
584	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
585	  \]
586	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
587	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
588	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
589	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
590	\end{definition}
591	
592	\begin{lemma}\leanok
593	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
594	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
595	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
596	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
597	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
598	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
599	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
600	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
601	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{lemma}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}\leanok
605	[Raising the pole order]
606	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
607	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
608	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
609	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
610	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
611	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
612	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
613	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
614	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
615	\end{lemma}
616	
617	\begin{lemma}\leanok
618	[Closure under difference]
619	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
621	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
622	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
623	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
624	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
625	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
626	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
627	\end{lemma}
628	
629	\begin{lemma}\leanok
630	[Closure under right shift]
631	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
632	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
633	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
634	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
635	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
636	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
637	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
638	  Lean.
639	\end{lemma}
640	
641	\begin{lemma}\leanok
642	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
643	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
644	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
645	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
646	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
647	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
648	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
649	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
650	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
651	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
652	\end{lemma}
653	
654	\begin{proof}
655	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
656	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
657	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
658	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
659	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
660	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
661	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
662	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
663	\end{proof}
664	
665	\begin{lemma}\leanok
666	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
667	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
669	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
670	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
671	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
672	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
673	  \[
674	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
675	  \]
676	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
677	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
678	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
679	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
680	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
681	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
682	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
683	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
684	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
685	\end{lemma}
686	
687	\begin{proof}
688	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
689	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
690	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
691	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
692	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
693	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
694	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
695	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
696	\end{proof}

698	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
699	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
701	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
702	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
703	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
704	  % Polynomials" (read from
705	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
706	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
707	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
708	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
709	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
710	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
711	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
712	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
713	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
714	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
715	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
716	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
717	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
718	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
719	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
720	  \[
721	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
722	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
723	  \]
724	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
725	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
726	\end{theorem}
727	
728	\begin{proof}
729	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
730	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
731	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
732	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
733	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
734	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
735	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
736	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
737	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
738	  function
739	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
740	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
741	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
742	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
743	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
744	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
745	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
746	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
747	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
748	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
749	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
750	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
751	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
752	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
753	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
754	\end{proof}

756	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
757	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
758	
759	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
760	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
761	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
762	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
763	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
764	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
765	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
766	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
767	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
768	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
769	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
770	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
771	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
772	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
773	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
774	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
775	
776	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
777	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
778	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
779	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
780	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
781	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
782	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
783	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
784	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
785	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
786	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
787	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
788	
789	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
790	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
791	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
792	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
793	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
794	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
795	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
796	\(x \in R_1\).

1029	\begin{definition}\leanok
1030	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
1031	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}

1038	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1039	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1040	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1041	  \begin{itemize}
1042	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1043	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1044	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1045	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1046	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1047	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1048	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1049	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),

1053	  Hilbert function} is
1054	  \[
1055	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1056	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},

1070	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1071	
1072	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1073	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1074	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1075	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1076	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1077	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1078	
1079	\begin{definition}\leanok
1080	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1081	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1082	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1083	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1084	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1085	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1086	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1087	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1088	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1089	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1090	\end{definition}
1091	
1092	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1093	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1094	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1095	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1096	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1097	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1098	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1099	\end{lemma}
1100	
1101	\begin{proof}
1102	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1103	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1104	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1105	\end{proof}
1106	
1107	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1108	[Degree-shift commutation]
1109	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1110	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1111	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1112	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1113	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1114	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1115	  \[
1116	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1117	  \]
1118	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1119	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1120	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1121	\end{lemma}
1122	
1123	\begin{proof}
1124	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1125	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous

1127	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1128	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1129	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1130	\end{proof}

1278	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1279	
1280	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1281	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1282	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1283	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1284	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1285	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1286	
1287	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1288	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1289	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1290	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1291	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1292	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1293	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1294	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1295	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1296	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1297	\end{lemma}
1298	
1299	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1300	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1301	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1303	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1304	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1305	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1306	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1307	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1308	\end{lemma}
1309	
1310	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1311	[The kernel pair nests]
1312	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1313	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1314	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1315	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1316	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1317	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1318	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1319	\end{lemma}
1320	
1321	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1322	[The cokernel pair nests]
1323	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1324	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1325	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1326	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1327	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1328	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1329	\end{lemma}
1330	
1331	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1332	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1333	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1335	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1336	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1337	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1338	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1339	\end{lemma}
1340	
1341	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1342	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1343	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1345	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1346	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1347	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1348	\end{lemma}
1349	
1350	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1351	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1352	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1353	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1354	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1355	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1356	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1357	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1358	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1359	\end{lemma}
1360	
1361	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1362	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1363	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1364	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1365	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1366	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1367	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1368	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1369	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1370	\end{lemma}
1371	
1372	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1373	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1374	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1375	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1376	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1377	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1378	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1379	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1380	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1381	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1382	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1383	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1384	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1385	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1386	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1387	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1388	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1389	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1390	  \[
1391	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1392	    \qquad
1393	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1394	  \]
1395	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1396	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1397	  \begin{enumerate}
1398	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1399	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1400	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1401	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1402	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1403	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1404	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1405	  \end{enumerate}
1406	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1407	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1408	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1409	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1410	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1411	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1412	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1413	\end{lemma}
1414	
1415	\begin{proof}
1416	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1417	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1418	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1419	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1420	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1421	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1422	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1423	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1424	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1425	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1426	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1427	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1428	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1429	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1430	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1431	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1432	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1433	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1434	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1435	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1436	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1437	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1438	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1439	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1440	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1441	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1442	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1443	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1444	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1445	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1446	
1447	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1448	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1449	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1450	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1451	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1452	
1453	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1454	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1455	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1456	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1457	
1458	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1459	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1460	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1461	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1462	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1463	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1464	\end{proof}

1477	  % NOTE: The Lean proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through a

1479	  %   finrank κ ↥(S.comap p.subtype) = finrank κ ↥(p ⊓ S), identifying the dimension
1480	  %   of the preimage of S under the inclusion of p with that of the ambient
1481	  %   intersection p ⊓ S. It is intentionally `private` (an implementation detail of
1482	  %   this identity) and carries no public blueprint node.
1483	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1484	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1485	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1486	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1487	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1488	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1489	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1490	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1491	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1492	  \[
1493	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1494	  \]
1495	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1496	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1497	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1498	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1499	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1500	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1501	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1502	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1503	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1504	\end{lemma}

1506	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1507	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1508	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1509	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1510	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1511	%  following short exact sequence
1512	%  $$
1513	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1514	%  $$
1515	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1516	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1517	\begin{proof}
1518	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1519	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1520	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1521	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1522	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1523	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1524	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1525	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1526	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1527	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1528	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1529	
1530	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1531	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1532	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1533	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1534	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1535	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1536	
1537	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1538	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1539	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1540	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1541	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1542	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1543	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1544	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1545	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1546	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1547	
1548	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1549	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1550	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1551	  \[
1552	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1553	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1554	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1555	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1556	  \]
1557	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1558	\end{proof}

1560	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1561	
1562	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1563	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1564	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1565	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1566	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1567	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1568	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1569	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1570	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1571	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1572	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1573	
1574	\begin{definition}\leanok
1575	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1576	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1578	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1579	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1580	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1581	  ring homomorphism}
1582	  \[
1583	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1584	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1585	  \]
1586	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1587	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1588	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1589	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1590	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1591	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1592	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1593	\end{definition}
1594	
1595	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1596	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1597	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1599	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1600	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1601	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1602	  \(t_i\).
1603	\end{lemma}
1604	
1605	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1606	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1607	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1608	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1609	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1610	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1611	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1612	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1613	\end{lemma}
1614	
1615	\begin{definition}\leanok
1616	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1617	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1618	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1619	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1620	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1621	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1622	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1623	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1624	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1625	  is measured.

1664	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1665	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}

1667	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
