1688	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1689	
1690	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1691	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1692	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1693	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1694	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1695	cokernel constructors.
1696	
1697	\begin{definition}
1698	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1699	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1702	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1703	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1704	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1705	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1706	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1707	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1708	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1709	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1710	  \[
1711	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1712	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1713	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1714	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1715	  \]
1716	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_{\,r}\) (indexed by
1717	  \(\mathrm{Fin.last}\,r\)) to \(0\) and each earlier variable
1718	  \(X_{\,\mathrm{castSucc}\,i}\) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants
1719	  (\(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{X_{\mathrm{castSucc}}}\),
1720	  \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{X_{\mathrm{last}}}\), \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_C\)).
1721	  It is a left inverse of the renaming map
1722	  \(\operatorname{rename}(\mathrm{castSucc}) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to
1723	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) (\(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{\mathrm{rename}\,\mathrm{castSucc}}\)),
1724	  and is therefore surjective (\(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{\mathrm{surjective}}\),
1725	  packaged as the \(\mathrm{RingHomSurjective}\) instance
1726	  \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{\mathrm{ringHomSurjective}}\)). This is the concrete
1727	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1728	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1729	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}); it is built with
1730	  multivariate evaluation, the \(\mathrm{Fin}\) last-case recursor, and the
1731	  variable-renaming map of \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}\).
1732	\end{definition}

1757	  \(t_i\), with the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carrying \(P\) into \(P'\)

1760	  \[

1762	      \;-\; \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t \circ \mathrm{castSucc})
1763	        \bigl(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}(s)\bigr)(m)

1823	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1824	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1825	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1826	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1827	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1828	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1829	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1830	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1831	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1832	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1833	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1834	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1835	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1836	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1837	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1838	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1839	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \(t \circ \mathrm{castSucc} =
1840	  (t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).

1842	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1843	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1844	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1845	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1846	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1847	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1848	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.

1851	\begin{proof}
1852	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1853	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1854	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1855	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1856	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1857	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and \(X_{\,\mathrm{castSucc}\,i}\)
1858	  to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1859	  \[
1860	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1861	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1862	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1863	  \]
1864	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1865	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1866	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1867	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1868	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1869	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1870	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1871	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1872	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1873	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1874	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1875	  \[
1876	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1877	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1878	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1879	  \]
1880	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1881	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1882	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1883	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).

1885	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1886	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1887	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1888	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1889	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1890	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1891	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1892	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1893	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1894	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1895	\end{proof}

1994	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1995	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1996	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1997	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1998	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1999	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}

2001	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2002	  \[
2003	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2004	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2005	  \]
2006	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2007	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2008	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2009	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2010	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2011	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2012	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2013	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2014	  \[
2015	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2016	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2017	  \]

2019	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2020	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.

2025	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}

2028	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2029	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2030	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)

2033	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +

2183	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2184	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2185	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2186	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2187	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2188	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2189	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2190	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2191	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2192	\begin{proof}
2193	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2194	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2195	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2196	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2197	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2198	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2199	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2200	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2201	
2202	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2203	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2204	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2205	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2206	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2207	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2208	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2209	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2210	
2211	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2212	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2213	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2214	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2215	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2216	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2217	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2218	  induction hypothesis applied
2219	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2220	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2221	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2222	  \[
2223	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2224	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2225	  \]
2226	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2227	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2228	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2229	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2230	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2231	\end{proof}

2233	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2234	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2235	
2236	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2237	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2238	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2239	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2240	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2241	
2242	\begin{definition}
2243	
2244	\leanok
2245	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2246	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
2247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
2248	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
2249	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2250	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2251	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2252	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2253	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2254	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2255	  % Polynomials" (read from
2256	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2257	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2258	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
2259	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
2260	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2261	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2262	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2263	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2264	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2265	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2266	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2267	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2268	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2269	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2270	  \[
2271	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2272	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2273	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2274	  \]
2275	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2276	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2277	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2278	
2279	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2280	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2281	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2282	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2283	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2284	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2285	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2286	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2287	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2288	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2289	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2290	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2291	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2292	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2293	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2294	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2295	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2296	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2297	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2298	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2299	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2300	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2301	
2302	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2303	\end{definition}

2326	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2327	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2328	  localization]
2329	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2330	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2331	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2332	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2333	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2334	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2335	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2336	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2337	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2338	  \[
2339	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2340	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2341	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2342	  \]
2343	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2344	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2345	
2346	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2347	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2348	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2349	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2350	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2351	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2352	\end{lemma}
2353	
2354	\begin{proof}
2355	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2356	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2357	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2358	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2359	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2360	
2361	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2362	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2363	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2364	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2365	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2366	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2367	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2368	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2369	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2370	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2371	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2372	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2373	\end{proof}

2495	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2496	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}

2501	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2502	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2503	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2504	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change

2645	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}

2648	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries

2789	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,

2795	\begin{proof}
2796	  
2797	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
2798	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
2799	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
2800	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
2801	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
2802	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
2803	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
2804	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
2805	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
2806	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
2807	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
2808	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
2809	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
2810	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
2811	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
2812	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
2813	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
2814	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
2815	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
2816	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
2817	
2818	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
2819	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
2820	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
2821	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
2822	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
2823	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
2824	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
2825	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
2826	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
2827	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
2828	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
2829	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
2830	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
2831	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
2832	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
2833	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
2834	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
2835	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
2836	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
2837	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
2838	\end{proof}

### 1. NOTE block in `lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff` (was lines 1477–1482)

**Issue:** Lean source identifiers used as prose: backtick-quoted `finrank_comap_subtype`, Lean type-ascription syntax `(p S : Submodule κ M)`, Lean terms `finrank κ ↥(S.comap p.subtype)` and `finrank κ ↥(p ⊓ S)`, and the Lean keyword `private`.

**Fix:** Replaced the note body with a purely mathematical description:
> "The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through a helper that identifies the dimension of the preimage of a submodule under the inclusion of an ambient submodule with the dimension of their ambient intersection. This helper is an implementation detail and carries no public blueprint node."

---

### 2. `def:graded_lastVarAlgHom` body prose (was lines 1716–1731)

**Issues (multiple):**
- `\(\mathrm{Fin.last}\,r\)` — Lean type name used as prose.
- `\(X_{\,\mathrm{castSucc}\,i}\)` — Lean `Fin.castSucc` used as index notation.
- Parenthetical clusters `(\(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{X_{\mathrm{castSucc}}}\), \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{X_{\mathrm{last}}}\), \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_C\))`, `(\(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{\mathrm{rename}\,\mathrm{castSucc}}\))`, `(\(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}_{\mathrm{surjective}}\), ...)` — Lean lemma names as prose (these are already covered by the `\lean{}` pins).
- `\(\mathrm{RingHomSurjective}\)` — Lean typeclass name.
- Last sentence about Lean implementation: "built with multivariate evaluation, the \(\mathrm{Fin}\) last-case recursor, and the variable-renaming map of \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}\)" — Lean construction detail.

**Fix:** Replaced with clean mathematical prose:
> "obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants. It is a left inverse of the algebra map \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\) on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete ring surjection … along which finiteness is transferred."

---

### 3. `lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem` — formula and prose (was lines 1762–1774)

**Fix:**

- Prose: replaced `t \circ \mathrm{castSucc}` with `(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})`.
- Proof description: replaced the `castSucc`-indexed case and `Fin.last` parenthetical with: "the case of an earlier variable \(s = X_i\) (\(i < r\)) is the inductive hypothesis after \(\mathrm{lastVarAlgHom}\) fixes it; the case of the last variable \(s = X_r\) annihilates the reduced term."

---

### 4. `lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer` — statement and proof (was lines 1839–1840, 1857)

**Issues:**

**Fix:**
